initiatives.baseline: 5 named agents (Alex, Sarah, Morgan, Marcus, Priya) + no generic agent. Current rendering: renderDesk() uses ctx.fillText for a single-character initial (line 117), 2x ctx.arc for brand-color dot (3px radius) and status dot (2.5px radius). No pixel-art sprites, no offscreen canvas caching, no drawImage calls anywhere in the codebase. Agent brand colors defined in AGENT_BRAND_COLORS (5 entries: blue, purple, green, orange, pink). Status dot colors in STATUS_DOT_COLORS (5 states). TILE_SIZE=40 world-space pixels. The initiative specifies 16x24 pixel-array templates — these would render inside the 40x40 desk tile.
initiatives.findings: 1. renderer.ts has a clean TILE_RENDERERS dispatch table (Record<TileType, TileRenderer>) at line 197 with 10 entries. Only renderDesk uses TileRenderOpts (agentName, brandColor, statusColor). 2. Agent character rendering is purely ctx.fillText of first letter (line 117: `ctx.fillText(opts.agentName[0], ...)`), plus two arc() calls for dots. No pixel art exists anywhere. 3. The TileRenderer signature is `(ctx, x, y, size, opts?) => void` — character sprites will need either (a) an expanded opts with a pre-rendered offscreen canvas, or (b) a module-level sprite cache keyed by agent+status that renderDesk looks up internally. Option (b) is cleaner — keeps the TileRenderer signature stable. 4. CanvasOffice already sets `ctx.imageSmoothingEnabled = false` (line 110), which is essential for crisp pixel-art upscaling. 5. OFFICE_AGENTS defines exactly 5 agents — the initiative mentions 6 (adding 'generic=gray'). Generic agent doesn't exist in OFFICE_AGENTS or AGENT_CHAR_MAP. The sprite system will need a fallback for unknown agent names. 6. OfficeGrid.tsx is dead code — never imported anywhere (only a comment reference in renderer.ts and CanvasOffice.tsx). Was the old CSS-grid implementation replaced by CanvasOffice. 7. The grid is 20x14 = 280 tiles, with only 5 desk tiles occupied by agents. Sprite caching at 1x scale is correct — each sprite is rendered once to an offscreen canvas, then drawImage'd at zoom scale. With imageSmoothingEnabled=false, integer-zoom upscaling will give crisp pixel art.
initiatives.recommended_approach: 1. Create `src/components/game/engine/sprites.ts` — new module for sprite definitions and caching. Define 16x24 pixel arrays as typed constants (e.g., `const ALEX_SPRITE: number[][]` using palette indices). Include a palette map per agent (brand color + skin + accent). 2. Implement a sprite cache: `Map<string, HTMLCanvasElement>` keyed by `{agentName}-{status}`. Each entry is a 16x24 offscreen canvas created via `document.createElement('canvas')`. Render pixel array to offscreen canvas once at 1x scale using fillRect per pixel. Status indicator (working/waiting/idle/error/offline) can be a small overlay or palette swap. 3. Export a `getAgentSprite(name: string, status: AgentStatus): HTMLCanvasElement` function. On first call for a given key, renders the sprite to an offscreen canvas and caches it. Subsequent calls return the cached canvas. 4. In renderer.ts, modify `renderDesk()` to call `getAgentSprite()` and use `ctx.drawImage(sprite, x + offset, y + offset, scaledW, scaledH)` instead of the current fillText+arc approach. The 16x24 sprite centered in a 40x40 tile leaves 12px horizontal and 8px vertical padding at 1x. 5. Keep the brand-color dot and status dot as-is (or integrate into sprite palette) — the dots are small and performant as arc() calls. 6. Add a generic gray sprite as the fallback for unrecognized agent names. 7. No changes needed to TileRenderer type signature, TileRenderOpts, TILE_RENDERERS dispatch table, camera.ts, or CanvasOffice.tsx — the change is fully contained within renderDesk() and the new sprites module.

follow_ups.action: Delete dead OfficeGrid.tsx — replaced by CanvasOffice, zero imports
follow_ups.rationale: File is never imported anywhere. Only referenced in comments. Safe to remove.

follow_ups.action: Remove stale OfficeGrid comment references in renderer.ts line 10 and CanvasOffice.tsx line 2
Add 'generic' agent color entry to AGENT_BRAND_COLORS (gray: '#9ca3af') for fallback sprite
follow_ups.rationale: Additive change, existing fallback already uses '#9ca3af' as default

initiatives.baseline: 10 tile types defined in TileType union. 10 renderer functions in TILE_RENDERERS dispatch table. Current rendering breakdown: (a) FLAT COLOR ONLY (fillRect): renderFloor (1 fillRect), (b) FLAT + BORDER: renderWall (fillRect + strokeRect), renderWhiteboard (fillRect + strokeRect + fillText 'W'), (c) FLAT + TEXT/EMOJI: renderCeoDesk (fillRect + fillText '★'), renderConference (fillRect + fillText 'T'), renderMailbox (fillRect + fillText '📫'), renderBell (fillRect + fillText '🔔'), renderServerRoom (fillRect + fillText 'SRV'), renderDoor (fillRect + fillText '◉'). Total: 10 fillRect calls, 8 fillText calls (including 2 emoji), 2 strokeRect calls, 2 arc calls (desk only). All tile types use the same TileRenderer signature. No Canvas path drawings, no procedural textures, no gradients exist yet.
initiatives.findings: 1. All 10 renderers follow the same pattern: fillRect for background, then optional text/emoji overlay. This is the simplest possible rendering — every tile is a flat colored rectangle. 2. The furniture tile renderers (desk, ceo-desk, conference, whiteboard, mailbox, bell, server-room, door) currently use fillText for icons. The initiative asks to replace these with procedural Canvas path drawings (moveTo/lineTo/arc/bezierCurveTo). 3. Floor and wall tiles are purely flat fills. The initiative asks for 'subtle texture passes' — this could be noise patterns, grid lines, or dithering rendered to cached tiles. 4. TILE_COLORS constant (line 13) maps all 10 tile types to hex colors. This will still be useful as base colors under the procedural details. 5. The dispatch table pattern (TILE_RENDERERS at line 197) is perfect for this work — each renderer function is independently replaceable without touching the render loop or any other renderer. 6. Performance concern: floor tiles dominate the grid (280 tiles, ~200 are floor). If floor texture involves per-pixel operations (noise), it MUST be cached to an offscreen canvas. The same applies to wall tiles (~56 tiles on perimeter). Furniture tiles (8 unique types, ~15 tile instances) are few enough that per-frame procedural drawing is acceptable, but caching is still recommended for consistency. 7. The render() function already has viewport culling (lines 247-250), so only visible tiles are drawn — this limits the worst-case draw count. 8. ctx.imageSmoothingEnabled = false is set in CanvasOffice — this is correct for pixel-art style but needs consideration for procedural paths (anti-aliased curves may look jagged). If the initiative wants smooth curves on furniture, imageSmoothingEnabled may need to be true for the procedural pass, then reset.
initiatives.recommended_approach: 1. Replace each renderer function in-place in renderer.ts. The TILE_RENDERERS dispatch table and TileRenderer type signature stay exactly the same — only the function bodies change. 2. For furniture tiles (desk, ceo-desk, conference, whiteboard, mailbox, bell, server-room, door): Replace fillText icon rendering with Canvas 2D path drawings. E.g., renderDesk draws a desk surface with a monitor shape (rectangles + bezier), renderServerRoom draws rack-mount shapes (stacked rectangles with LED dots), renderMailbox draws a mailbox outline, etc. Keep base fillRect as ground color, overlay procedural paths on top. 3. For floor tiles: Add a subtle texture pass after the base fillRect — options include (a) a repeating 4x4 pixel noise tile cached to an offscreen canvas and drawn via createPattern(), or (b) thin 1px grid lines at tile boundaries. Floor is ~70% of tiles so caching is mandatory. Create a single offscreen canvas for the floor texture pattern at init time. 4. For wall tiles: Add subtle brick/stone texture — horizontal lines every 8-10px with offset vertical joints. Can be drawn procedurally since wall tile count is moderate (~56 tiles), or cached like floor. 5. Create a `src/components/game/engine/textures.ts` module for offscreen texture caching (floor pattern, wall pattern). Export cached CanvasPattern objects that renderers can use via `ctx.fillStyle = pattern`. 6. Keep TILE_COLORS as the base palette — procedural details are drawn on top of the base color. 7. The imageSmoothingEnabled=false setting in CanvasOffice works well for pixel-art textures but may cause jagged edges on curved paths. Consider: (a) stick with rectilinear paths only (pixel-art aesthetic), or (b) temporarily enable smoothing for specific renders. Recommend option (a) for visual consistency with the pixel-art sprite initiative.

follow_ups.action: Audit imageSmoothingEnabled interaction with procedural curves — if furniture sprites use arcs/beziers, test at all 4 zoom levels for visual quality
follow_ups.rationale: Could produce jagged artifacts at certain zoom levels; easy to revert by toggling smoothing per-renderer

follow_ups.action: Performance benchmark floor texture rendering at zoom level 4 (all 280 tiles visible, 4x scale) — measure frame time before and after
follow_ups.rationale: Floor tiles dominate the grid; texture rendering must not regress from current <1ms frame time. Offscreen caching mitigates this but needs verification.

follow_ups.action: Delete dead OfficeGrid.tsx (same as character-sprites follow-up)
follow_ups.rationale: File is never imported anywhere. Safe to remove.
